description: Signal diagnostics, HART/Fieldbus issues, transmitter failures, control valve problems, analyzer faults, temperature/level/flow measurement troubleshooting, wiring issues, and DCS/PLC-side problems for field instrument technicians

zero_mA_open_circuit.symptom: DCS shows BAD or no reading, mA meter reads 0.0 mA
causes: Broken wire anywhere in the loop (field to JB, JB to marshalling, marshalling to I/O card)
Blown fuse in loop power supply or at marshalling cabinet
Disconnected terminal (wire fell out, terminal block screw backed off from vibration)
Bad transmitter power supply (no 24VDC at transmitter terminals)
Terminal block cracked or corroded (invisible failure — ohm check shows open)
Wrong I/O card channel or card pulled for maintenance
troubleshooting_steps: 1. At transmitter: measure DC voltage across power terminals — should see 12-24VDC depending on loop resistance. If 0V, problem is upstream (power supply or wiring).
2. Check fuse at marshalling cabinet — 80% of open circuits are blown fuses
3. Trace the loop with a multimeter on continuity: transmitter -> JB -> marshalling -> I/O card
4. Check for wire pulled out of terminal (tug test each wire gently)
5. If transmitter has power but no output: transmitter is dead — swap with spare
zero_mA_open_circuit.gotcha: On some systems, the DCS alarm says 'sensor failure' when the real problem is a blown fuse 200 feet away in the marshalling cabinet. Don't start replacing sensors without checking the loop first.

below_3_6mA_downscale_burnout.symptom: DCS shows below-range or downscale burnout alarm, mA reads less than 3.6 mA
causes: Sensor failure — open thermocouple, broken RTD element, failed sensor in transmitter
Open TC junction in connection head (loose terminal, corroded crimp)
Extension wire broken inside insulation (looks intact but ohms out open)
Transmitter configured for downscale burnout on sensor failure (this is working as designed)
Wrong TC type connected (Type J wire to Type K transmitter — reads completely wrong)
troubleshooting_steps: 1. At transmitter: check if transmitter diagnostics show sensor failure (HART communicator or local display if equipped)
2. Disconnect sensor wires at transmitter and measure: TC should show millivolts, RTD should show ohms (100 ohms at 0C for Pt100)
3. If sensor reads good at transmitter terminals: problem is in transmitter configuration or transmitter itself
4. If sensor reads open at transmitter: trace back to connection head, check each junction
5. For RTDs: check all leads — 3-wire RTD with one broken compensating lead reads low
below_3_6mA_downscale_burnout.gotcha: Downscale burnout is the DESIGNED behavior for many safety applications — before troubleshooting, confirm the sensor actually has a problem. A tech once replaced 6 transmitters before realizing the process was actually below range.

above_21mA_upscale_burnout.symptom: DCS shows above-range or upscale burnout alarm, mA reads greater than 21 mA
causes: Sensor failure with transmitter configured for upscale burnout
Shorted sensor input (moisture in TC head, wire insulation failure)
Process actually above transmitter range (check if range is set correctly for current conditions)
Transmitter electronics failure (output stage saturated)
troubleshooting_steps: 1. Verify process is within transmitter range — is the vessel actually overpressure or is the range wrong?
2. Check transmitter diagnostics via HART for specific error code
3. Disconnect sensor and apply known input from calibrator — if transmitter reads correctly, sensor is the problem
4. Check for moisture in connection head or JB (shorted thermocouple wires read ambient temperature and transmitter may drive upscale)

erratic_signal.symptom: DCS reading jumps around, noisy trend, intermittent spikes or dropouts
causes: Loose connection (terminal screw not tight, wire nicked and making intermittent contact)
Ground loop (shield grounded at both ends — stray current adds noise to signal)
EMI/RFI from nearby VFDs, motors, power cables, radio transmitters, welding
Moisture in junction box or connection head (corrosion creates variable resistance)
Damaged cable insulation (intermittent short to ground or other pair)
Bad terminal block (hairline crack creates variable contact)
troubleshooting_steps: 1. Look at the DCS trend — pattern tells you the cause:
- 60 Hz ripple (smooth oscillation): EMI from power cables
- Random spikes: loose connection or intermittent short
- Slow drift with occasional jumps: moisture or corrosion
- Signal follows motor start/stop: EMI from VFD or motor
2. Check all terminal connections — retorque, look for green corrosion, check for moisture
3. Verify shield grounded at ONE end only (marshalling cabinet end) — disconnect shield at field end if grounded there
4. Check cable routing — 12-inch minimum separation from power, 90-degree crossings only
5. Temporarily jumper around each section of the loop to isolate which segment has the problem
6. If near a VFD: check for proper cable shielding and consider adding a filter or moving the cable
erratic_signal.gotcha: The most common 'erratic signal' is a loose wire that the tech retorques and says 'it's fixed' — but it comes back in a week when vibration loosens it again. Use ferrules on stranded wire in screw terminals.

signal_pegged_4_or_20.symptom: Reading stuck at exactly 4.00 mA (0%) or 20.00 mA (100%), does not change with process
causes_pegged_at_4: Transmitter in manual mode or local override (output forced to 4 mA)
Process actually at zero (empty vessel, no pressure, no flow)
Transmitter output manually set to 4 mA for testing and never returned to auto
Plugged impulse line (no process signal reaching transmitter — reads zero DP)
Equalizing valve left open on DP transmitter (equalizes H and L — reads zero DP)
causes_pegged_at_20: Transmitter in manual mode output forced to 20 mA
Process actually at 100% or above range
Plugged low-side impulse line on DP transmitter (sees full static pressure as DP)
Transmitter range set too narrow for actual process (0-10 PSI range on a 50 PSI process)
troubleshooting_steps: 1. Check if transmitter is in AUTO or MANUAL mode (HART communicator or local display)
2. Check if DCS output is forced or overridden (DCS operator check)
3. For DP transmitters: check manifold valve positions — block valves open, EQ valve closed
4. Check actual process conditions — is the reading actually correct?
5. For plugged impulse lines: bleed the high side and low side, watch if reading changes

need_250_ohm_resistor.why: HART communicates by superimposing a 1200/2200 Hz FSK signal on the 4-20 mA DC loop. The 250-ohm resistor provides enough impedance for the HART modem to detect the signal.
need_250_ohm_resistor.where: In series in the loop — most systems have this at the DCS I/O card (input impedance of the AI card). If not, add an external 250-ohm precision resistor.
need_250_ohm_resistor.problem_without_it: HART modem cannot communicate — you get 'no device found' or 'communication error'
need_250_ohm_resistor.check: Measure total loop resistance. Must be 230-600 ohms for reliable HART. Below 230 = add resistor.

minimum_loop_current.requirement: Loop current must be above 3.5 mA for HART communication — below this, there isn't enough power for the transmitter to operate and communicate
minimum_loop_current.problem_scenario: Transmitter in alarm/burnout driving 3.6 mA or less — HART can't connect, and you can't read diagnostics
minimum_loop_current.workaround: Use a HART communicator with its own power supply, or temporarily increase loop power

modem_connection_point.correct: Connect HART modem leads across the transmitter terminals (+ to + and - to -) or across the 250-ohm resistor
modem_connection_point.wrong: Do NOT connect across the power supply terminals — the power supply has very low impedance and absorbs the HART signal
modem_connection_point.in_junction_box: Connect at the transmitter-side terminals in the nearest JB for best signal quality
modem_connection_point.at_marshalling: Can connect at marshalling cabinet terminals — but long cable runs attenuate the signal, may not work reliably

minimum_loop_impedance.range: 230-600 ohms total loop impedance for reliable HART communication
minimum_loop_impedance.too_low: Below 230 ohms: add external resistor to bring into range
minimum_loop_impedance.too_high: Above 1100 ohms: HART signal too attenuated, transmitter may not have enough voltage to operate

common_hart_error_codes.no_device_found: Loop impedance too low, modem connected wrong, transmitter not HART-capable, or transmitter dead
common_hart_error_codes.communication_error: Noisy environment (EMI), long cable run, poor connections, multiple devices on multidrop without proper addressing
common_hart_error_codes.device_busy: Transmitter is performing internal operation (self-test, writing to EEPROM) — wait and retry
common_hart_error_codes.command_not_supported: HART revision mismatch — communicator DD (device description) doesn't match transmitter revision. Update DD library.
common_hart_error_codes.write_protected: Transmitter write protection enabled (hardware jumper or software lock) — check for write protect switch on transmitter

multidrop_issues.description: Multiple HART devices on one pair — all outputs forced to 4 mA, communication only via HART polling
multidrop_issues.max_devices: Up to 15 devices on one loop (HART 7), but practically limited to 4-6 for reliable communication
multidrop_issues.common_problem: Each device must have a unique polling address (1-15). Address 0 = standard 4-20mA operation. If two devices have the same address, neither communicates.
multidrop_issues.rarely_used_because: Slow update rate (seconds per device) and loss of 4-20 mA analog signal — mostly used for HART IP applications now

segment_design.max_devices_per_segment: Up to 32 addressable, but practically 8-16 devices per H1 segment depending on power budget and scheduled communications

cable_length_limits.trunk_maximum: 1900 meters (6200 feet) with Type A cable
cable_length_limits.total_spur_length: Sum of all spurs on segment must not exceed limits per number of devices
cable_length_limits.cable_type_A: Single twisted pair, shielded, 18 AWG — the ONLY cable type recommended for new installations

power_calculations: Each H1 device draws 15-25 mA at 9-32VDC (typical 20 mA)
Segment power supply provides 24-32VDC with current limiting (350 mA typical)
Total current budget: power supply capacity minus cable voltage drop losses
Calculate voltage at LAST device on segment — must be above 9VDC minimum for device to operate

h1_vs_hse.H1: 31.25 kbit/s — field-level bus connecting instruments. Power and communication on same pair. This is what the instrument tech deals with.
h1_vs_hse.HSE: 100 Mbit/s — High Speed Ethernet backbone connecting H1 segments to the control system via linking devices. Network engineer territory.

junction_box_configuration: Fieldbus junction boxes contain device couplers (short-circuit protection per spur)
Each spur connects one device — device coupler isolates faults on that spur from the trunk
Terminate the trunk at both ends with proper terminator (100 ohm resistor + 1 uF capacitor in each terminator)
Exactly TWO terminators per segment — no more, no less. Missing or extra terminators cause communication failures.
Spare spurs capped but device coupler powered for future use

topology.trunk_spur: Most common: main trunk cable runs through junction boxes, individual spurs drop to each device. Preferred for maintenance — one device fault doesn't kill the segment.
topology.daisy_chain: Devices connected in series on the trunk. Simpler cabling but one fault can affect all downstream devices. Avoid in new installations.
topology.tree: Multiple branches from a single junction point. Acceptable if properly terminated.
topology.star: NOT recommended — creates impedance mismatches and signal reflections

diagnostic_tools: Fieldbus diagnostic handheld (Fluke, Pepperl+Fuchs, or similar) — checks voltage, noise, signal quality, device list
Physical layer diagnostic: check DC voltage (must be 9-32V at device), noise level (must be below threshold), signal amplitude
Live device list: see which devices are communicating, check for devices falling off the bus (intermittent connection)
Segment wiring test: check for shield continuity, short circuits, open circuits, and cable length measurement using TDR
common_fieldbus_problems: Missing or extra terminator — causes signal reflections and intermittent communication
Trunk cable too long — voltage drops below 9V at far end, devices brown out
Shield grounded at both ends — creates ground loop that adds noise to the bus
Wrong cable type (using standard instrument cable instead of Type A fieldbus cable) — impedance mismatch causes signal degradation
Device firmware mismatch — older device blocks segment communication until firmware updated or device removed

overpressure_damage.symptom: Reading pegged high or reading shifted permanently after pressure spike
overpressure_damage.cause: Process pressure exceeded transmitter maximum working pressure — diaphragm deformed or ruptured
check: Look at process connection for visible diaphragm damage (if accessible)
Apply known pressure from calibrator — if transmitter won't zero or won't span correctly, diaphragm is damaged
HART diagnostics may show 'sensor failure' or 'electronics failure' or out-of-range pressure readings
overpressure_damage.fix: Replace transmitter — overpressure damage is not repairable in the field
overpressure_damage.prevention: Install overpressure protection (snubber, needle valve, or blowout plug) on transmitters in pulsating or spike-prone services

plugged_impulse_lines.symptom: Slow or no response to process changes, reading drifts to zero DP, readings frozen
plugged_impulse_lines.cause: Process material (scale, wax, slurry, ice, hydrate) has plugged the impulse line or root valve
check: Bleed the impulse line at the transmitter — does fluid flow freely?
Close block valves, open bleed, apply air/N2 from transmitter end — can you blow back to process?
If both sides plugged on DP transmitter: reads zero. If only one side plugged: reads pegged high or low.
fix: Rod out with proper size wire (from transmitter end toward process, never from process end into transmitter)
Blow through with N2 at regulated pressure (not full bottle pressure — that damages the transmitter)
For chronic plugging: install seal pots, diaphragm seals, or purge systems
Heat trace impulse lines in cold climates where freezing causes plugging
plugged_impulse_lines.gotcha: Never rod out impulse lines from the process side toward the transmitter — you push debris into the transmitter diaphragm and destroy it. Always work from transmitter toward process.

wet_leg.symptom: Level reading drifts slowly, reads higher or lower than actual level over days/weeks
wet_leg.cause: Fill fluid in reference leg evaporating, leaking, or absorbing process vapor — changes reference leg density
wet_leg.fix: Refill reference leg to correct level with specified fill fluid (glycol, silicone oil, or process condensate per design). Check for leaks at every fitting in the reference leg.

dry_leg.symptom: Level reads higher than actual, especially in cold weather or after rain
dry_leg.cause: Condensation collecting in the dry reference leg — adds weight that looks like process level
dry_leg.fix: Drain the reference leg completely. Install drain valve at low point. In cold/humid environments, consider converting to wet leg or capillary system.

diaphragm_coating.symptom: Slow response, reading offset, transmitter doesn't return to zero when process is drained
diaphragm_coating.cause: Process material coated or deposited on the sensing diaphragm (common in slurry, polymer, and waxy services)
diaphragm_coating.check: Remove transmitter from service, inspect diaphragm face. Any visible coating = problem.
diaphragm_coating.fix: Clean diaphragm carefully per manufacturer instructions (usually soft cloth and solvent — NEVER scrape or use abrasives). If coating is hardened, transmitter may need factory service or replacement.
diaphragm_coating.prevention: Use diaphragm seal with flush-mount or extended diaphragm for dirty/coating services

stiction.symptom: Valve oscillates or hunts around setpoint, limit cycles in control loop, valve position jumps in steps instead of smooth movement
stiction.cause: Static friction in packing or guide surfaces — valve sticks, controller output builds up, valve breaks free and overshoots, sticks again in new position
diagnosis: Plot controller output vs valve position — stiction shows as a 'staircase' pattern on the position signal
Valve positioner diagnostics (if smart positioner): look for friction signature in valve scan/travel test
Step test: command small step changes (1-2%) — valve with stiction won't move until step exceeds static friction breakaway
fix: Adjust or replace packing — this is the most common cause. Check packing torque, replace if hardened or worn.
If guides are scored or corroded: pull valve for shop repair
As a temporary control fix: increase deadband in positioner (reduces hunting but also reduces accuracy)
Consider switching to low-friction packing (PTFE, graphite with live-loading)

hysteresis.symptom: Valve position is different at the same controller output depending on direction of travel (going up vs coming down)
hysteresis.cause: Mechanical backlash in linkage, loose connections between positioner feedback and valve stem, or elastic deformation in actuator spring/diaphragm
hysteresis.diagnosis: Full stroke test up and down — plot command vs position. Gap between up-stroke and down-stroke curves = hysteresis. Should be less than 1-2% of travel.
fix: Tighten positioner feedback linkage — any play in the connection translates directly to hysteresis
Check actuator spring for fatigue or permanent set
Verify positioner calibration and feedback range match actual valve travel
For severe cases: valve internals may be worn (guide/plug clearances excessive)

seat_leakage.symptom: Valve commanded closed but flow continues, downstream temperature/pressure doesn't reach expected shutoff value
seat_leakage.cause: Damaged seat or plug (erosion, cavitation damage, debris trapped in seat), insufficient actuator force to fully seat, worn soft seat insert
diagnosis: Command valve to 0% — measure leakage rate if possible (upstream pressure, downstream flow measurement)
Listen for flow noise with a stethoscope or ultrasonic leak detector at the valve body when fully closed
Check actuator bench set — is spring providing enough seating force?
fix: Increase actuator spring pre-load if seat force is insufficient (adjust spring range)
Replace trim (plug and seat) if eroded or damaged — this requires pulling the valve from the line
For soft-seated valves: replace the seat insert (PTFE, PEEK, etc.)
Check for debris: cycle valve fully open and closed several times to flush debris, then retest

leakage_classes.Class_II: 0.5% of full capacity — general purpose, not tight shutoff
leakage_classes.Class_IV: 0.01% of full capacity — standard for most process control valves
leakage_classes.Class_V: 5 x 10^-4 ml/min per inch of seat diameter per PSI differential — near tight shutoff
leakage_classes.Class_VI: Bubble tight — zero visible leakage. Required for safety shutoff valves. Metal-to-metal requires lapping.

air_leaks.symptom: Valve drifts from position, positioner constantly adjusting, air consumption increased, hissing sound
air_leaks.check: Apply snoop/soap to all pneumatic connections: tubing fittings, diaphragm casing, cylinder packing, positioner connections
air_leaks.fix: Replace leaking fitting, tighten packing, replace diaphragm or O-rings as needed

diaphragm_tear.symptom: Valve fails to correct position (spring return), slow response, air exhausting continuously from diaphragm vent hole
diaphragm_tear.check: Look for air exhausting from the underside of the diaphragm case (there is usually a vent hole). Any air coming out = torn diaphragm.
diaphragm_tear.fix: Replace diaphragm — this is a field-repairable job but requires proper diaphragm kit and following the actuator manual for reassembly torque and clamping

spring_failure.symptom: Valve doesn't reach full open or full close, fail position test fails, valve sluggish
spring_failure.cause: Spring fatigue, permanent set (spring compressed for years), or wrong spring installed after maintenance
spring_failure.check: Measure bench set (air pressure required for full stroke) — compare to nameplate. If way off, spring is the problem.
spring_failure.fix: Replace spring with correct range per actuator sizing. Verify bench set after replacement.

feedback_linkage_loose.symptom: Position feedback oscillates, positioner output hunts, DCS shows different position than physical valve
feedback_linkage_loose.check: Physically inspect linkage from valve stem to positioner feedback arm — any play, looseness, or misalignment
feedback_linkage_loose.fix: Tighten linkage, replace worn pins/bearings, realign feedback arm to match full travel

calibration_drift.symptom: Valve doesn't reach 0% or 100% at command endpoints, zero offset, span error
calibration_drift.fix: Recalibrate positioner: set zero at valve closed, set span at valve full open. Follow positioner-specific procedure (varies significantly by manufacturer).

ip_converter_failure.symptom: No air output from positioner despite correct input signal, or air output doesn't change with signal
ip_converter_failure.cause: I/P (current-to-pneumatic) converter in positioner failed — common on older positioners and in dirty/wet air environments
ip_converter_failure.check: Verify 4-20 mA input is reaching I/P converter. Measure output air pressure — should change proportionally.
ip_converter_failure.fix: Clean I/P nozzle/flapper (if accessible), replace I/P module, or replace entire positioner. Ensure instrument air is clean and dry to prevent recurrence.

sample_system_blockage.symptom: No flow through sample system, analyzer reading frozen or drifting to zero
sample_system_blockage.cause: Plugged sample probe, clogged filter, blocked sample line, failed sample pump
troubleshooting: Check sample flow indicator/rotameter — is there flow?
Check sample filter differential pressure — high DP = clogged filter, replace element
Check sample pump operation (if installed) — motor running? Diaphragm intact?
Flush sample line from probe end with clean solvent or gas per procedure
Check sample conditioning system (cooler, knockout pot, pressure regulators) for blockage or failure

probe_fouling.symptom: Slow response time, reading drift, reading doesn't match grab sample
probe_fouling.cause: Process material deposited on sensor probe (pH electrode coating, oxygen sensor fouling, conductivity cell buildup)
fix: Clean probe per manufacturer procedure (specific cleaning solution for each probe type)
pH probes: soak in appropriate cleaning solution (acid for scale, bleach for biological), check reference fill
O2 probes: replace membrane and electrolyte
If cleaning doesn't restore response: replace the sensor element

calibration_gas_expired.symptom: Analyzer fails auto-calibration, calibration error alarm, readings drift between calibrations
calibration_gas_expired.cause: Calibration gas concentration has degraded past its certified shelf life (reactive gases degrade faster than inerts)
calibration_gas_expired.fix: Replace with fresh certified calibration gas. Check expiration dates on ALL gas bottles — a common audit finding.
calibration_gas_expired.note: Reactive gases (H2S, SO2, NO) can degrade significantly in 6-12 months. Non-reactive (CH4, CO2, N2) stable for 2-3 years. Always use within certified period.

response_time_degraded.symptom: Analyzer takes much longer than normal to respond to process changes, validated against grab sample timing
causes: Sample transport time increased (long sample line, low sample flow, partially blocked line)
Sensor fouled or aged (slower diffusion through fouled membrane or aged electrode)
Sample conditioning delay (cooler working slowly, filter restricting flow)
Analyzer in calibration cycle or diagnostic mode (temporarily unresponsive to process)
response_time_degraded.fix: Address root cause: increase sample flow rate, clean/replace sensor, service sample conditioning, verify analyzer mode

reagent_depleted.symptom: Analyzer alarm for reagent low, colorimetric analyzer reads zero or erratic, titration analyzer fails
reagent_depleted.fix: Refill reagent per manufacturer procedure. Use correct reagent grade and concentration. Check tubing for cracks or leaks in reagent delivery system.

tc_burnout_detection.symptom: Temperature reading goes to upscale or downscale burnout, alarm in DCS
check: At transmitter or connection head: disconnect TC wires, measure millivolts with a multimeter
Open circuit (infinite resistance between TC leads) = burned out or broken TC
If millivolts are present and reasonable: problem is in the transmitter or wiring downstream of the TC head
Heat the thermowell slightly (heat gun on exterior) — mV should increase. No change = TC is dead.
common_causes: Thermocouple element degraded from high temperature or chemical attack
Wire broken inside thermowell (vibration fatigue at the transition from sheath to wire)
Loose terminal in connection head (not burnout, just disconnected — tighten and retest)

rtd_drift.symptom: RTD reading slowly diverges from actual temperature over weeks/months, doesn't match nearby TC or other measurement
rtd_drift.cause: RTD element contamination, insulation resistance breakdown, or lead wire degradation
diagnosis: Compare RTD reading to a reference TC installed at the same point — should agree within 1-2 degrees under stable conditions
Measure RTD resistance at connection head: Pt100 should read 100 ohms at 0C, 138.5 ohms at 100C
Check insulation resistance between RTD leads and sheath — should be above 100 megohms
If resistance is correct but reading is wrong: check for 2-wire vs 3-wire vs 4-wire configuration and lead resistance
rtd_drift.fix: Replace RTD element if drift is confirmed and consistent. For slow drift, recalibrate and monitor — if it drifts again quickly, replace.

broken_thermowell.symptom: Process leakage at thermowell, temperature reading changes to ambient, visible damage
check: SAFETY FIRST — process leakage at a thermowell is a potential hazard (hot fluid, toxic, flammable). Follow plant isolation/emergency procedures.
Look for process fluid weeping from the thermowell top/connection head
If thermowell fractured, the sensor is exposed to process and readings may be erratic or correct but you have a containment breach
Vibration-induced fatigue at the weld or at the step change in diameter is the typical failure mode
broken_thermowell.fix: Isolate the process connection. Replace the thermowell. Perform a wake frequency calculation if vibration was the cause — thermowell may need a shorter length or heavier wall.

open_circuit_in_head.symptom: Reading goes to burnout, same as TC burnout but the TC element itself is fine
check: Open the connection head and visually inspect: look for loose wires, corroded terminals, moisture damage
Tug test each wire — if a crimp joint breaks, that's your problem
Check for moisture/condensation — connection head heaters or proper sealing prevents this
Verify correct wire type in head (not someone's field splice with wrong extension wire)
open_circuit_in_head.fix: Re-terminate the connection. Replace crimp connectors. Dry out the connection head. Install head heater if condensation is chronic.

foam.symptom: Level reads higher than actual, fluctuates with process agitation
foam.cause: Foam on liquid surface reflects radar signal or affects DP measurement
foam.fix: Switch to guided wave radar (GWR) which measures through foam, use nuclear (gamma) level for severe foam, or install anti-foam injection

agitation.symptom: Level reading oscillates with agitator rotation, noisy signal
agitation.cause: Surface turbulence creates variable reflections (radar) or DP oscillation
agitation.fix: Install stilling well/still pipe, increase signal damping, use GWR in stilling well, position radar antenna away from agitator

heavy_vapor.symptom: Radar level reads higher than actual, signal strength reduced
heavy_vapor.cause: Dense vapor layer above liquid changes the propagation speed of the radar signal
heavy_vapor.fix: Use GWR (guided signal not affected by vapor), apply vapor compensation factor if available in transmitter configuration, switch to DP level

radar_antenna_fouling.symptom: Signal lost, false echoes, reading jumps to zero or maximum
radar_antenna_fouling.check: Inspect antenna for process buildup — remove instrument from nozzle if safe, or use purge air to clear
fix: Clean antenna with appropriate solvent
Install PTFE-lined antenna face (process slides off instead of sticking)
Install purge air connection to keep antenna clear
For severe fouling: switch to GWR with coated probe or to non-contact nuclear
radar_antenna_fouling.gotcha: Some condensation on the antenna is normal in steam or hot liquid service. The transmitter has algorithms to ignore near-echoes. But heavy coating overwhelms this.

dp_level_reference_leg_imbalance.symptom: Level reading slowly drifts from actual level, doesn't return to zero when vessel is empty
causes: Wet leg fluid level has changed (evaporation, leak, wrong fill level after maintenance)
Dry leg has accumulated condensate
Specific gravity of process fluid changed (different product, temperature change)
Reference leg leaking at a fitting (slow leak = slow drift)
troubleshooting: Verify wet leg is filled to correct level — check fill procedure and verify at sight glass if equipped
Check all reference leg fittings for leaks with snoop/soap
Verify current process specific gravity matches the calibration value — recalibrate if SG has changed
For dry leg: drain any accumulated condensate from the low point
Verify transmitter zero with vessel empty (or at known level) — re-zero if offset

displacer_level_issues.symptom: Level reads wrong, oscillates, or doesn't respond to level changes
causes: Displacer stuck in cage (process buildup, mechanical damage)
Torque tube damaged or fatigued (reading offset or no response)
Process specific gravity changed from calibration value
Displacer weight wrong for service (after maintenance, wrong displacer installed)
troubleshooting: Verify displacer moves freely — drain the chamber and manually move the displacer, check for binding
Check torque tube for visible damage, corrosion, or deformation
Verify current specific gravity matches data sheet — SG decrease = reads lower than actual, SG increase = reads higher
With chamber empty: transmitter should read 0%. With chamber full: should read 100%. If not, recalibrate with current SG.

orifice_plate_fouling.symptom: Flow reading drifts low over time, DP decreases even though flow hasn't changed
orifice_plate_fouling.cause: Buildup on the upstream face of the orifice plate increases the effective bore diameter, reducing DP for the same flow
orifice_plate_fouling.check: Compare DP transmitter reading to expected DP at known flow rate. If DP is low, suspect fouling.
orifice_plate_fouling.fix: Pull the orifice plate and inspect/clean. For chronic fouling, consider switching to a flow meter less susceptible to buildup (venturi, V-cone, or averaging pitot tube).
orifice_plate_fouling.note: Fouling on the DOWNSTREAM face is less critical but can affect accuracy. Both sides should be cleaned.

reynolds_number_effects.symptom: Flow reading inaccurate at low flow rates, reads high compared to actual at very low flow
reynolds_number_effects.cause: Orifice plate discharge coefficient changes at low Reynolds numbers (laminar/transitional flow regime) — the orifice equation assumes turbulent flow
reynolds_number_effects.check: Calculate Reynolds number at the low flow condition. If Re < 10,000 at the orifice, accuracy is degraded.
reynolds_number_effects.fix: For services that operate at low flow frequently: use a smaller bore orifice (gives higher DP at low flow) or switch to a flow meter with better low-flow accuracy (Coriolis, turbine, or vortex).

pulsation_effects.symptom: Erratic DP readings, DP transmitter readings oscillate with a regular frequency matching pump or compressor speed
pulsation_effects.cause: Pulsating flow from reciprocating pumps, compressors, or reciprocating engines
fix: Install pulsation dampener (snubber) on the impulse lines between the orifice taps and the transmitter
Increase impulse line length (acts as a natural dampener)
Install a capacity tank near the meter to reduce pulsation
Increase DP transmitter damping setting (but this slows response — OK for totalizing, bad for control)
Best solution: move the meter to a location farther from the pulsation source

cavitation_downstream_of_meter.symptom: Flow reading unstable, reading higher than actual, noise from downstream valve or piping
cavitation_downstream_of_meter.cause: Control valve downstream of flow meter creates backpressure fluctuations that affect meter accuracy
cavitation_downstream_of_meter.fix: Install flow meter upstream of the disturbance with adequate straight run, or relocate the meter. For orifice plates: minimum 8D downstream to control valve.

mag_meter_empty_pipe.symptom: Reading jumps erratically or shows phantom flow when pipe is empty or partially full
mag_meter_empty_pipe.cause: Electromagnetic flow meter requires full pipe to read correctly — partially full pipe gives wrong cross-sectional area
mag_meter_empty_pipe.check: Verify pipe is always full at meter location. Check empty pipe detection feature (most modern mag meters have this — verify it's enabled).
mag_meter_empty_pipe.fix: Relocate meter to a point in the system that is always full (downstream of a back-pressure valve, vertical run flowing upward, or bottom of a U-bend).

coriolis_tube_coating.symptom: Mass flow reading drifts over time, density reading shifts, meter diagnostics show tube frequency change
coriolis_tube_coating.cause: Process material coating the inside of the measuring tubes changes the effective mass and resonant frequency
coriolis_tube_coating.check: Run meter zero verification at no-flow condition. If zero has shifted significantly, coating is likely.
coriolis_tube_coating.fix: Clean tubes with appropriate cleaning solution per manufacturer procedure (CIP — clean in place). If density reading has shifted, the coating is heavy enough to require mechanical cleaning or tube replacement.

ground_loops.symptom: 60 Hz hum/noise on signal, signal oscillates or has offset, noise gets worse when equipment starts nearby
ground_loops.cause: Instrument cable shield grounded at BOTH ends — current flows through the shield between the two ground points, inducing noise on the signal wires
ground_loops.rule: Ground shield at ONE end only — at the marshalling cabinet (system end). Float the shield at the field end.
fix: Locate and disconnect the field-end shield ground. Tape/insulate the disconnected shield end.
Verify no accidental shield grounding from cable gland contact, conduit contact, or tray contact
After fixing: check signal with a multimeter for noise. Should be steady DC with less than 10 mV of AC noise.
ground_loops.exception: Some project specs require shield grounded at both ends for lightning protection on long outdoor runs — use an approved shield ground kit with capacitor/resistor to ground at the field end, which drains lightning while blocking 60 Hz.

emi_rfi_interference.symptom: Signal noise increases near motors, VFDs, power cables, radio transmitters, or welding operations
prevention_rules: Maintain 12-inch minimum separation from power cables (24 inches from VFD cables)
Cross power cables at 90 degrees only — NEVER run parallel in same tray
Use shielded cable for all instrument signals
Instrument cable in dedicated tray or conduit separate from power
For severe EMI: use twisted pair cable (twisting cancels induced noise)
fix: Reroute cable away from EMI source if possible
Add ferrite chokes (clip-on ferrite) to the cable near the noise source
Verify shield integrity and single-point grounding
For VFD interference: ensure VFD output cable is shielded with shield grounded at both ends at the VFD (THIS is the exception — VFD output shields are grounded at both ends)

moisture_ingress.symptom: Intermittent signal problems especially after rain, corrosion on terminals, green/white deposits on connections, insulation resistance drops
check: Open junction boxes and inspect for water/condensation inside
Check cable glands — all tight? Correct size for cable?
Check conduit drains at low points — are they installed and functional?
Measure insulation resistance (megger) between conductors and between conductors and shield — should be above 20 megohms
fix: Dry out the junction box (heat gun carefully, or leave open in dry weather with heater installed)
Replace corroded terminals and wire ends — cut back wire to clean copper and re-terminate
Apply anti-oxidant compound (Burndy Penetrox or equivalent) to connections in outdoor/humid environments
Tighten all cable glands to proper torque, replace any damaged seals
Install junction box heaters in humid environments (small thermostatically controlled heaters prevent condensation)

corroded_connections.symptom: Increased loop resistance, signal offset, intermittent readings, visible corrosion at terminals
corroded_connections.check: Inspect all terminal blocks, especially in outdoor and coastal environments. Look for green (copper), white (aluminum), or red (steel) corrosion.
fix: Cut back wire to clean copper (minimum 1/4 inch beyond any discoloration)
Clean terminal block contacts with appropriate cleaner (electrical contact cleaner, NOT WD-40)
Apply anti-oxidant compound to all connections before retorquing
Replace entire terminal block if corrosion has pitted the contact surfaces
For aluminum wire in marshalling cabinets: ALWAYS use anti-oxidant, aluminum oxide creates high resistance and intermittent connections

wrong_scaling.symptom: DCS displays wrong engineering units or wrong value for a known input. Transmitter reads correct mA but DCS shows wrong pressure/temperature/level.
wrong_scaling.cause: DCS point configured with wrong URV (upper range value) or LRV (lower range value) — does not match transmitter calibrated range
wrong_scaling.example: Transmitter calibrated 0-100 PSI (4 mA = 0, 20 mA = 100). DCS configured 0-200 PSI. At 50 PSI actual, transmitter sends 12 mA, DCS reads 100 PSI. Everything 'works' but every reading is double the actual value.
fix: Compare transmitter range (from data sheet or HART) to DCS channel configuration (from DCS engineer)
They MUST match exactly: same LRV, same URV, same engineering units
After fixing: inject 4 mA and 20 mA from calibrator, verify DCS reads LRV and URV exactly
Check square root extraction for DP flow — should be enabled at transmitter OR DCS, NOT both (double square root = readings too low at low flow, too high at high flow)

alarm_suppression_left_on.symptom: Alarm condition exists but no alarm annunciates in DCS — alarm was suppressed during testing/commissioning and never re-enabled
alarm_suppression_left_on.check: In DCS alarm summary, look for suppressed/inhibited/shelved alarms. Check alarm management system for any global suppression still active.
alarm_suppression_left_on.fix: Remove alarm suppression. Review ALL alarms in the system for suppression status — this is a common audit finding and safety issue.
alarm_suppression_left_on.note: Alarm suppression during loop checks is normal. But a procedure must exist to remove ALL suppressions before turnover. A master alarm suppression audit should be done before start-up.

bad_io_channel.symptom: One channel on an I/O card gives wrong reading or no reading, other channels on the same card are fine
check: Swap the field wiring to a known-good spare channel on the same card — if the reading is now correct, the original channel is bad
Check for channel-level diagnostics in the DCS (most modern systems report individual channel faults)
Verify no crossed wiring at the terminal block (wrong pair landed on the channel)
fix: Move wiring to spare channel and update DCS configuration to reflect the new channel number
Replace the I/O card if no spare channels are available
Document the channel change in as-built wiring drawings

module_addressing_error.symptom: New or replaced I/O module not recognized by DCS, or wrong module appears at a node address
module_addressing_error.cause: DIP switches or software addressing on the I/O module don't match the DCS configuration database
fix: Verify module address matches the DCS configuration — check DIP switches on the module against the system documentation
For HART I/O or Fieldbus: verify the device address in the DCS database matches the physical device address
After replacing a module: confirm the replacement has the same firmware version and configuration — some systems require explicit module registration

configuration_mismatch.symptom: Field instrument and DCS disagree on range, units, alarm points, or fail-safe behavior
configuration_mismatch.cause: DCS engineer configured the point based on one revision of the data sheet, field tech calibrated per a different revision, or changes were made in the field without updating the DCS configuration
prevention: Single source of truth: instrument index/data sheet is the master — both field and DCS must calibrate/configure to the SAME document and SAME revision
After any field change: notify DCS engineer to update configuration
After any DCS change: notify field to verify transmitter range still matches
Pre-startup check: systematically verify every point's field calibration matches the DCS configuration — this is tedious but catches mismatches that cause start-up delays
common_mismatches: Transmitter range changed in field but DCS not updated (or vice versa)
Alarm setpoints changed in DCS but not reflected in control narrative
Burnout direction set differently in transmitter vs DCS (both drive upscale but DCS expects downscale = wrong alarm response)
Engineering units mismatch (transmitter in PSIG, DCS in kPa — no one notices until readings make no sense)
